mcqs.question: Which of the following is a necessary and sufficient condition for an undirected graph to have an Eulerian circuit?

options.text: All vertices have even degree
All vertices have odd degree
Exactly two vertices have odd degree
The graph is complete

mcqs.explanation: For an undirected graph to have an Eulerian circuit, every vertex must have even degree. This is both necessary and sufficient for connected graphs.

mcqs.question: In a directed graph, when does an Eulerian path exist?

options.text: All vertices have equal in-degree and out-degree
At most one vertex has (out-degree - in-degree) = 1 and at most one has (in-degree - out-degree) = 1
All vertices have out-degree greater than in-degree
The graph is strongly connected

mcqs.explanation: For a directed Eulerian path, at most one vertex can have out-degree exceeding in-degree by 1, at most one can have in-degree exceeding out-degree by 1, and all others must have equal in/out-degree.

mcqs.question: Which problem is known to be NP-complete?

options.text: Finding an Eulerian circuit
Finding a Hamiltonian path
Determining if a graph is connected
Finding the minimum spanning tree

mcqs.explanation: Finding a Hamiltonian path is NP-complete, while the other problems can be solved in polynomial time.

mcqs.question: According to Dirac's theorem, a simple graph with n vertices (n ≥ 3) has a Hamiltonian cycle if:

options.text: Every vertex has degree at least n/2
The sum of degrees of any two non-adjacent vertices is at least n
The graph is complete
The graph is bipartite

mcqs.explanation: Dirac's theorem states that if each vertex has degree at least n/2, then the graph contains a Hamiltonian cycle.

mcqs.question: Which graph property distinguishes Eulerian from Hamiltonian problems in terms of computational complexity?

options.text: Eulerian problems focus on edges while Hamiltonian focus on vertices
Eulerian paths can be found in polynomial time while Hamiltonian paths are NP-complete
Eulerian circuits require connected graphs while Hamiltonian cycles don't
Hamiltonian problems are always solvable while Eulerian problems are not

mcqs.explanation: The key distinction is computational complexity: Eulerian paths/circuits can be found in O(E) time, while Hamiltonian paths/cycles are NP-complete.
